questions.question_text: Under RCW 18.85.285, who is responsible for performing monthly reconciliation of the trust account?
questions.explanation: RCW 18.85.285 requires the designated broker to perform monthly reconciliation and monthly trial balances of the trust account. This responsibility cannot be delegated to other licensees.

questions.question_text: A Washington brokerage opens a trust account at an out-of-state bank offering higher interest rates. Is this permissible?
questions.correct_answer: No, must be at a WA financial institution
distractors: Yes, if the bank is FDIC insured
questions.explanation: Washington law requires that pooled interest-bearing trust accounts be maintained at a Washington state financial institution. Using an out-of-state bank is not permitted regardless of insurance status or interest rates.

questions.question_text: A designated broker deposits $500 of personal funds into the firm's trust account to cover bank fees. What violation has occurred?
questions.correct_answer: Commingling of personal and trust funds
questions.explanation: Commingling personal funds with trust account funds is prohibited under Washington law. Even depositing personal funds to cover bank fees constitutes commingling. Proper procedures must be followed for fee payments.

questions.question_text: How often must a Washington designated broker perform trial balances on the trust account?
questions.explanation: Under RCW 18.85.285, the designated broker must perform both monthly reconciliation and monthly trial balances of the trust account. This ensures ongoing accuracy and accountability of all entrusted funds.

questions.question_text: Trust account records for a Washington brokerage firm must be maintained by whom?
questions.explanation: Washington law requires that trust account records be maintained by the firm. The designated broker oversees the account, but record-keeping is a firm-level responsibility under RCW 18.85.285.

questions.question_text: A buyer provides a $7,500 earnest money deposit. Into which type of account must this deposit be placed?
distractors: A separate trust account for the buyer
questions.explanation: Deposits of $10,000 or less must go into the firm's pooled trust account. Since $7,500 is under the $10,000 threshold, it must be deposited in the pooled account. Interest from pooled accounts goes to the WA Housing Trust Fund.

questions.question_text: A client provides a $25,000 earnest money deposit. What must the broker provide to the client?
distractors: A receipt only with no options
questions.explanation: For deposits over $10,000, the broker must give written notice that the client may choose between a pooled trust account (interest goes to the state) or a separate trust account (interest goes to the client).

questions.question_text: Interest earned on funds in a Washington pooled trust account is distributed to which entities?
questions.correct_answer: Housing Trust Fund and RE Education Account
distractors: The brokerage firm retaining the funds
The Department of Licensing general fund
questions.explanation: Interest from pooled trust accounts goes to the Washington Housing Trust Fund and the Real Estate Education Program Account. This funds affordable housing initiatives and real estate education programs statewide.

questions.question_text: A client deposits $15,000 in earnest money and chooses a separate trust account. Who receives the interest earned?
questions.explanation: When a client deposits over $10,000 and elects a separate trust account, the interest earned belongs to the client. This is the key difference from the pooled account, where interest goes to the state.

questions.question_text: What is the deposit threshold that triggers a Washington broker's obligation to offer the client a separate trust account option?
questions.explanation: The $10,000 threshold is the dividing line. Deposits of $10,000 or less must go into the pooled account. Deposits over $10,000 require the broker to give written notice of the client's right to choose between pooled and separate accounts.

questions.question_text: A broker receives an earnest money check on Saturday afternoon. When must the broker deposit the funds?
questions.explanation: Washington law requires earnest money to be deposited by the next banking day following receipt, unless the purchase agreement allows a deferred deposit. Since Saturday is not a banking day, the deposit must be made on the next available banking day.

questions.question_text: A purchase agreement states that earnest money will be deposited within 5 business days. The broker receives the check on Tuesday. By when must the funds be deposited?
questions.correct_answer: Within 5 business days per the agreement
distractors: By Wednesday, the next banking day
questions.explanation: While the default rule requires deposit by the next banking day, a purchase agreement may allow a deferred deposit schedule. When the agreement specifies 5 business days, the broker must follow that contractual timeline.

questions.question_text: Both buyer and seller claim entitlement to a $12,000 earnest money deposit after a failed transaction. What must the designated broker do first?
distractors: Immediately return funds to the buyer
Split the funds equally between parties
File an interpleader action in court
questions.explanation: When earnest money is disputed, the designated broker must first notify all parties in writing. The broker then has up to 30 consecutive days after notification to disburse the funds according to the resolution.

questions.question_text: After a designated broker sends written notification of a disputed earnest money deposit, what is the maximum time allowed before disbursement?
questions.explanation: Washington law requires that disputed earnest money funds be disbursed no later than 30 consecutive days after the designated broker's written notification to all parties. This prevents indefinite holding of disputed funds.

questions.question_text: A broker holds earnest money for a transaction that falls through. The buyer demands return but the seller objects. The designated broker sends written notice. Forty-five days pass with no resolution. Has the broker violated Washington law?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, funds must be disbursed within 30 days
distractors: No, there is no time limit for disputes
No, 45 days is within the 60-day limit
Only if neither party files a lawsuit
questions.explanation: The designated broker has violated the 30-consecutive-day disbursement deadline. After written notification, funds must be disbursed within 30 consecutive days. Holding funds for 45 days exceeds this statutory requirement.
